data.title: Universal Corp: Diversification Driving Our Buy Call
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Nightbloom Analysis as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Universal Corporation (NYSE:UVV) is a global leader in the leaf tobacco industry, operating across more than 30 countries. Universal sources and processes tobacco and other plant-based ingredients for consumer product manufacturers and benefits from the global supply chain and existing relationships with major consumer goods companies. Because of that, UVV is a critical player in the agricultural products sector, especially in the tobacco supply chain.

Over the next 1-3 years, Universal's outlook is shaped by two strategic drivers. First, Tobacco Operations are positioned for margin stability as supply-demand conditions normalize, which should support steady cash generation and dividend coverage. Second, the Ingredients segment offers structural growth potential as new capacity and acquisitions ramp up, diversifying revenues and unlocking operating leverage. The stock price has been flat year-over-year, which may not be in line with these developments and indicates that the market isn't fully appreciating this company's potential.

The Company In A Nutshell

Universal Corporation operates through two primary segments.

Tobacco Operations

This segment sources, processes, and sells leaf tobacco to the world's largest cigarette manufacturers, such as Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), Japan Tobacco, Inc. (OTCPK:JAPAY), and Imperial Brands PLC (IMMBY). One of the company’s biggest strengths is working directly with farmers. The main tobacco varieties handled are flue-cured, burley, and oriental, which are diversified by region and end-use. The company processes the leaves to customer specifications, manages quality control, and delivers them. Even though this segment has declined, it is still the backbone of the company, as it generates more than 75% of revenue.

Ingredients Operations

Universal has made several strategic moves to diversify into plant-based ingredients. They did this through acquisitions such as Silva International (dehydrated fruits and vegetables), Shank's Extracts (vanilla and other natural flavors), and FruitSmart (fruit juices and concentrates). Through this segment, different food, beverage, and flavor companies are customers that use those products in packaged foods, beverages, and more. Universal’s strategy is to use the tobacco model to source crops, process them at scale, and provide logistics. The Ingredients Operations isn't contributing that much to revenues yet, but it is expanding and has a higher long-term margin potential.

What Recent Results Tell Us

Universal reported its first quarter results over the period ending June 30, 2025, with revenue of $594 million. This is a slight decrease compared to the previous quarter. However, the company achieved a significant change in profitability, with operating income rising to $34 million from $17 million. Net income was $8.5 million, or $0.34 per diluted share.

The main driver of performance was the Tobacco Operations segment, which generated $504.7 million in revenue. The revenue of the segment is down 8% y-o-y because of a decrease in carryover crop sales. However, due to a favorable product mix in Asia and improved pricing strategies, operating income for the segment rose significantly. This mix was driven by higher demand for flue-cured leaf from African and Asian suppliers, which has higher margins than burley tobacco. The sales, particularly to Japan Tobacco and other Chinese buyers, helped offset weaker burley volumes in North America.

On the other hand, the Ingredients Operations segment saw a decline in operating income due to a less favorable product mix and tariff uncertainties. This creates a short-term drag on margins, with two broader implications. First, these pressures highlight the execution risk that comes with bringing in a new business line, where external factors can weigh on profitability, such as trade policy. Second, these margin pressures could slow the revenue growth of the Ingredients segment.

From a valuation standpoint, this creates a short-term cap on multiple expansion. The market is probably discounting the growth of Ingredients until the company shows consistent profitability. Investors are cautious as margins are under pressure from tariffs and higher costs. Until the company demonstrates sustained profitability in the segment, the market will keep discounting the growth. However, with successfully resolving tariff uncertainties or improving the product mix, the trajectory could shift, which supports stronger revenues.

On the balance sheet, cash increased by $76 million quarter-over-quarter, which led to a cash balance of $178 million. Long-term debt is at $618 million, which results in a debt-to-equity ratio of 41%. Even with elevated leverage, the company could still meet interest obligations from operating income. The balance sheet shows adequate near-term liquidity and great interest coverage, offset by substantial leverage, which could negatively affect the financial flexibility if crop conditions or global demand were to weaken.

In terms of cash flow, the company continues to reflect the seasonal nature of the tobacco business. Regularly, free cash flow is often negative in the first half of the fiscal year, as the company’s focus is on building inventory and extending working capital to suppliers. As expected, this was the case for Q1 too, where working capital requirements absorbed a big part of operating cash. Management typically offsets this cyclicality with credit facilities, which is the reason for the high debt levels. That said, Universal has always converted earnings into positive free cash flow over the full year, especially in the second half of the year. In that period, crop deliveries will accelerate, which will lead to receivables being converted to cash. The business model has proven capable of generating enough annual cash to cover dividends and service debt.

Two Paths Ahead: Tobacco vs. Ingredients

Universal’s management gave clear directional guidance for fiscal year 2026. The company is expecting sales of around $1.35-1.45 billion, which is 2-9% lower than in the fiscal year 2025. Also, it is expected to see tobacco move to a more balanced position as crop size will increase by 25-45% over the fiscal year. With current crop sizes, flue-cured and burley tobacco will be in oversupply positions by the end of the fiscal year. Flue-cured tobacco is commonly used in cigarette blends, which make up the biggest portion of the volumes. Burley, on the other hand, is a lighter leaf that adds specific flavor notes. Both of them are core revenue drivers, which means that oversupply could directly pressure their pricing. Management also stated that the continued progress will support existing and new Universal Ingredients customers with the resources and expanded facilities.

The acceleration of the plant-based ingredients platform is very important. The company is building scale with acquisitions, which indicates a real move to leverage processing and logistics skills in higher-margin and growing markets. Universal offsets the decline in tobacco demand while opening potential in promising markets. Universal could capture a meaningful slice of the growing demand for natural flavors and plant-based ingredients with its existing processing and logistics expertise. Revenue streams will be diversified, profitability will improve, and reliance on tobacco will decline.

Another important factor of the expansion is the diversification and leveraging of existing farming and logistics capabilities. Looking at sweet potatoes as an example, Universal can apply the same sourcing, processing, and logistics model proven in tobacco to ingredients. Management has already highlighted sweet potatoes and other specialty crops as examples where its sourcing and processing model can be replicated, a strategy reinforced by recent acquisitions in plant-based ingredients. This model includes direct farmer contracting, agronomic support, and centralized processing before distribution. Universal could ensure quality and consistency by applying this model to sweet potatoes while still scaling production. This then reduces the exposure to the decline of tobacco. Importantly, this strategy aligns with the broader consumer and food industry trends towards natural ingredients, suggesting that Universal's capabilities give it a credible path to grow outside its core market.

In the Tobacco Operations segment, ongoing market normalization could rebalance supply and demand. This would lead to more stable margins if volume management and the product mix remain well executed. The company therefore has to keep inventories aligned with demand and secure contracts with buyers on good terms. Also, the company has to adjust sourcing between flue-cured and burley in line with demand, which leads to stabilized revenues and protected margins. With the Ingredients segment, Universal could benefit from expanded capacity and a broader product portfolio. The segment has potential for stronger operating leverage and margin gains. These dynamics show the company is well-positioned to safeguard and strengthen its competitive standing.

What Could Go Wrong?

Next to these promising developments within its Tobacco segment, there are a few headwinds, too. The possible tobacco oversupply could put pressure on pricing power and margins over time. Also, regulatory or demand shocks could strengthen the decline of tobacco, which reduces cash flow that supports dividends.

On the Ingredients segment side, there are risks to consider as well. Think about the tariff-related uncertainty, but also the elevated fixed costs due to the expansion. With its acquisitions, operations need to be integrated across geographies, cultures, and product lines. Pressure on margins could be the case if there are problems maintaining quality, harmonizing systems, or scaling the production efficiently. If leverage increases, financial flexibility will be reduced if crop conditions deteriorate or cash generation falls below expectations. If these risks were to negatively affect margins or slow down the growth of Ingredients, the valuation discount would move more towards a Hold stance.

Putting Valuation in Perspective

The financial position looks solid with a Debt/EBITDA ratio of 3.7x, which means moderate leverage while still maintaining operational resilience. Also, the company is delivering consistent capital returns, with a dividend yield of around 5.8% and a payout ratio of ~79%. This reinforces investor confidence in the company’s cash generation capabilities.

With regard to valuation metrics, the company is currently trading at an EV/EBITDA of 7.87x, which is well below the 5-year historical median of 9.02x. It is also below the sector median of 12.21x, which is a positive sign, too. The P/E ratio is at 13.5x, which is around the 5-year average but significantly lower than the 10-year average of 17.6x. This suggests that the stock is currently priced with caution, even with stable fundamentals. Historically, when the company last traded at such valuation levels, the company had more leverage, fewer shareholder return programs, and less diversified revenue streams. Today, the company is strengthened by the expanding and diversifying plant-based ingredients operations, robust free cash flow generation, and a great income profile. The company continues to focus on expanding the Ingredients segment, which supports a long-term valuation argument with the sustainable dividend and financial discipline.

The company’s free cash flow yield over the full year (FY 2025) stands at an impressive 18.8%, with $259 million in free cash flow over the whole year and $1.4 billion in market cap. This is significantly higher than the 5-year average of ~2%. The yield shows how the company is improving its cash generation capabilities, especially in the current market environment.

In terms of profitability, the company had an EBITDA margin of above 10%. This is also a great number, but a bit lower than the sector median. However, this is not as simple as that, as Universal is not a pure tobacco player anymore, which means it can not be compared to 1 sector only. The numbers show Universal’s operational efficiency and strategic positioning within the sector.

Why UVV Is a Buy

Universal Corporation is well-positioned for the medium/long term, combining the Tobacco Operations segment with a rapidly expanding Ingredients segment. The company has a lot of experience and knowledge about sourcing, processing, and logistics. This expertise can be leveraged to capture growth in plant-based ingredients and natural flavors. With the move to reduce reliance on tobacco, revenue streams will diversify. Universal is strategically investing in expanding capacity and operational efficiency, which strengthens its competitive edge. With these developments, Universal is aiming for sustainable profitability, enhancement of margins, and long-term shareholder value. The current valuation offers a reasonable and attractive value proposition for investors, as there is low leverage, strong cash flow, and a diverse business mix. This supports a Buy recommendation.

data.title: Friedman Industries: Coming To Life; Interesting To Watch
data.description:
Shares of Friedman Industries (NASDAQ:FRD) have seen some real momentum in recent weeks. This followed a strong first-quarter earnings report and a substantial acquisition here, igniting momentum in the shares.

As the past performance of the business is cyclical, driven by industry dynamics, I am cautious to chase the shares here. However, I look forward to learning more about the current margins and recent M&A actions in the coming months to potentially reconsider this neutral stance.

A Metalworking Business

Friedman Industries was founded in 1965 and is a Texas-based business that operates 7 locations, which include six flat-rolled coil processing facilities and a tubular operation that produces licensed electric resistance welded pipe mills.

The company has been listed since the early 1970s and processed nearly 600,000 tons of metal products in 2025, generating over $400 million in revenues in the process. The company focuses on the processing part of the supply chain by cutting, slitting, and stretching raw materials after buying metals in bulk from mills. All this is done in order to provide tailor-made and just-in-time deliveries to customers.

Of course, these products are supplied to a wide range of industries as the company focuses heavily on risk management activities and digital experiences for its customers. The company, however, is just a very small player in the metal service center industry, which is led by Reliance (RS) with a 7% market share, as 75% of this market is served by smaller businesses like Friedman and many others.

The company has been looking to consolidate as well, as tons shipped more than doubled in 2023 as a result of the acquisition of Plateplus' East Chicago and Granite City facilities, while it commissioned a new internal facility as well.

This expansion and growth meant that the business saw revenue grow from less than $100 million a decade ago to peak at over $500 million in the year ending in March 2023, after which sales have seen stagnation ever since, with much of the growth leading up to 2023 driven by pricing. Operating margins have fluctuated wildly from anywhere between roughly break-even levels to 10% of sales, amidst a rather flattish share count.

Picking Up The Valuation

In June of this year, the company reported a 14% fall in full-year sales to $445 million, as earnings from operations fell from $24 million to just $3 million. Aided by hedge gains, net earnings came in at $6.1 million, down from $17.4 million in the year before, with these earnings amounting to $0.87 per share.

Promising in this light was that fourth-quarter sales were down just over 2% to $129 million, with operating profits reported at $5.9 million, with the profit number surpassing the profit number for the entire year after steel prices recovered by year-end.

In August, Friedman reported a 17% increase (on an annual basis) in first-quarter sales to nearly $135 million, driven by pricing but mostly by a 12% increase in tons sold. Operating profits of $7.0 million (ahead of hedging results) were solid for single-digit margins, allowing profits to come in at $0.71 per share. This momentum is not fully expected to be maintained, as while second-quarter tons are seen up, prices are seen coming down a bit.

Net debt as of the first quarter of $31 million was limited as an outstanding share base of nearly 7 million shares traded around the $15 mark in August, granting the company a $136 million enterprise valuation. This all changed in recent weeks, as shares have rallied to the $22 mark here, setting fresh highs, thereby valuing the business at $185 million. This is equal to about 0.3-0.4 times sales here for a business that, in recent quarters, is posting margins in the mid-single digits.

Adding To The Momentum

The latest momentum, which took shares from $19 to $22 early in September, has been driven by the announcement of an acquisition, namely that of Century Metals & Supplies. Few financial details have been announced other than that the all-cash deal will be immediately accretive to earnings, with average revenues over the past three years amounting to $111 million. This suggests roughly 20-25% sales contribution to the firm.

Based on similar sales multiples as its own firm, the purchase price could amount to $30-$40 million, but perhaps more, as Century owns its own real estate. Given that little has been said, it has been surprising to see shares gain three dollars upon the announcement, as that amounts to $20 million in value creation amidst few details announced.

What Now?

Frankly, I am glad that I have learned about Century here, as it shows rapid growth, but despite the best intentions, this remains a very cyclical and volatile margin business after all. However, in recent quarters, there is real momentum apparent, pushing up earnings yields a great deal, as this deal could be interesting.

Given the lack of details shared on the contribution and impact of the Century deal here, I look forward to updating my take upon the release of the next earnings report, yet I am curious to see if this can become an interesting value creator in this space, just like industry leader Reliance Steel.

data.title: Rocket Lab: My Biggest Miss And Best Call Ever
data.description:
Introduction

This is one of those articles I wrote with bittersweet feelings.

On one hand, I am really happy my call paid off. On the other hand, I never invested in it, leaving me on the sidelines of my best call ever.

I'm obviously talking about the Rocket Lab Corporation (NASDAQ:RKLB), as the title of this article already gave away. A company I started covering on June 19, 2024, using the title "Rocket Lab: Ticket To Ride The Space Economy Boom." Since then, shares have risen by roughly 780%.

My most recent article was written on March 1. Since then, RKLB shares have doubled, which beats the market by 96 points, a mind-blowing number.

Needless to say, I regret not buying it. To stay sane, I am not even going to calculate what I would have made if I had actually bought RKLB last year with an average starter position.

All kidding aside, what I do not regret is staying true to my core strategy, which is investing in proven business models instead of startups, although it would be very fair if someone were to make the case that LandBridge Company LLC (LB), my biggest investment, breaks that rule.

This is what I wrote in my March 1 article with regard to my strategy:

[...] I will also stick with my message to conservative investors. Highly volatile equities may not be right for you. That's also why I do not own RKLB, as I am a dividend-focused investor who is already massively overweight in the defense sector - except that I own bigger contractors with less volatility and much higher market caps.

With that said, my four defense contractors, which are all included in the chart below and account for roughly a fifth of my article, also did well, as the entire group beat the S&P 500 by a substantial margin, with General Electric Company (GE) returning 66%. The worst performer was Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC), which still saw a return of 25%.

While there are certainly some aerospace & defense players that haven't done so well, the market has clearly woken up to the potential of this sector, especially of emerging players like Rocket Lab Corp.

Hence, in this article, I'll update my Rocket Lab thesis and explain what to make of the risk/reward after the most mind-blowing performance I've ever seen.

So, let's get to it!

A Startup Gem Turning Into A Disruptor

In 2021, Rocket Lab went public through a reverse merger with a special purpose acquisition company called Vector Acquisition Corp. Back then, the market was truly a crazy place, as rates were low, the Fed was doing QE, and investors were buying "anything" with a good growth story.

That also explains why so many companies that went public back then turned into horrible investments, as investors were overpaying and massively overestimating the disruptive power of some of these SPAC deals.

Rocket Lab wasn't one of them.

While its stock price got crushed in a decline of roughly 80% when the market entered a bear market in 2022, it quickly recovered and is now trading at more than 4x the price investors paid the moment it went public in 2021.

See, Rocket Lab has two things going in its favor:

  • It benefits from a massively growing market. According to McKinsey & Company (via Mitrade), the space economy will be $1.8 trillion by 2035. That would be triple the current size and offer massive potential.
  • Rocket Lab is actually in a terrific spot to capture growth. The problem is that many people often assume that a large addressable market implies that a company in that area will do well. That is wrong. If I squeeze a lemon and add some water and sugar, I'm not automatically benefiting from The Coca-Cola Company's (KO) $1.3 trillion addressable market. I apologize for this silly comparison, but it's an often-made mistake to use an addressable market as a selling point. Yet many companies keep doing it, as it works to get (potential) investors excited.

As we can see below, in 2024, the total space economy was estimated to be valued at roughly $600 billion, which would align with the outlook of McKinsey & Company, as it's a third of the expected size in 2035.

Being successful in this market depends on a few factors, as SpaceNews reported, including the ability to deliver cutting-edge technologies to grow one's market share, as well as good relationships with government agencies, which are, by far, the biggest drivers of growth for now.

As the space economy approaches a trillion-dollar valuation, it is positioned at the intersection of technological innovation and market disruption. Emerging business models, such as Direct-to-Device services and in-orbit economies, hold promise for the industry’s future. However, their success will depend on early mission performance and commercial viability.

Government investment remains a key factor in driving growth, with global military space budgets exceeding $64 billion. Defense spending continues to outpace civilian expenditures, underscoring the strategic importance of space in national security and international competitiveness. - Spacenews

Rocket Lab has all of this, as it isn't just a mini version of SpaceX that builds long tubes with powerful engines at the bottom, but it's what it calls an end-to-end provider of space industry services. It offers a wide range of services, including satellite design, manufacturing, launch, and on-orbit operations that make it a one-stop shop for many customers, including the mighty NASA.

To add some more details, the company operates launch complexes in New Zealand (Launch Complex 1) and Virginia, USA (Launch Complex 3). It also has a history of reliable small launches with its Electron rocket, which is basically the foundation of its massive success in the first post-IPO years.

For some time, the company has been developing the reusable Neutron rocket to diversify launch options for national security and constellation deployments. Or to put it differently, successes in other areas have allowed it to take on much bigger projects, as its internal supply chain has proven to be successful.

Meanwhile, in space systems, it builds satellite components and supports missions for agencies like NASA, ESA, and the mighty U.S. Department of Defense ("DoD"), which almost everyone wants as a customer due to anti-cyclical demand and America's focus on winning the space race.

But that's still not everything, as Rocket Lab's strategy also includes pursuing large programs like the Golden Dome missile defense initiative and SDA constellations, which allow it to use its proven track record of keeping projects on time and below budget to bid on what I described as a potential $1 trillion program in a relatively recent article.

Another benefit is that all of this has translated to great financials.

Rocket Lab Is Firing On All Cylinders

In its latest financial release, the company noted it generated $144.5 million in 2Q25 revenues, beating the high end of its prior guidance with a 36% year-over-year growth rate, supported by substantial tailwinds in Launch Services and Space Systems. On a sequential basis, revenue rose by roughly 18%.

Even better, the company's margins are strong. As great as fast revenue growth is, margins are increasingly important for a wide range of reasons, including that investors want to see Rocket Lab prove it can efficiently manage its operations and work on a way to positive earnings. As of 2Q25, the company continues to lose money, as it saw a $23 million loss in operating cash flow and $32 million in CapEx. It reported an EBITDA loss of $28 million.

The good news is that the company is one of those non-profitable growth players with a terrific balance sheet, as it has more than $560 million in cash, north of $120 million in marketable securities, and less than $400 million in long-term debt, $346 million of which are convertible senior notes.

Moreover, in 2Q25, gross margins were 32.1%, slightly above the upper bound of the 30.0%-32.0% guidance range, while non-GAAP gross margins reached 36.9%, 90 basis points above the company's guidance upper bound, supported by higher average selling prices of the Electron program, a favorable selling mix in Space Systems, and tailwinds from higher-margin component sales.

It ended the quarter with a backlog of roughly $1.0 billion, 58% of which will likely be turned into revenues over the next 12 months, with the remaining 42% ending up on its income statement top line in the 12 months after that.

This brings me to the next part.

The Future Remains Bright

Rocket Lab has reached the stage where it isn't just growing organically but using its balance sheet to add acquired growth. This includes the takeover of Geost, which is a maker of missile-tracking satellites for national security purposes. Without knowing anything about the deal, we can assume it's a move to benefit from next-gen operations related to the Golden Dome missile shield, as tracking missiles is key to this program—or any major defense plan.

The deal also adds payloads to Rocket Lab's launch and spacecraft capabilities, further expanding its role as a one-stop shop for national security, as it brings an inventory of space-based missile warning sensors and manufacturing facilities in Arizona and Northern Virginia, which further expands its domestic supply chain, something that comes with additional benefits in a world of increasing deglobalization and tariff risks.

That's why founder Peter Beck was so upbeat about the Golden Dome project, as he knows he's now managing a company that goes well beyond getting payload to space:

The $175 billion Golden Dome program could prove to be one of DoD's largest procurements to date, and we're in a great position to capitalize on opportunities here. Our strategic investment and the way that we scale the company to uniquely meet its needs positions us strongly to win either as a prime contractor or even as a sub or even as a component supplier. Our pursuit of the Golden Dome extends just beyond payloads. Across its entire ecosystem, we have the technology and capability ready to serve. - RKLB 2Q25 Earnings Call

Beyond this, the core business is thriving, as its Electron rocket had 69 launches when the company reported its 2Q25 earnings. By the end of the year, it aims to increase that number by another 20 launches, fully supported by contracts from multiple space agencies and international demand.

In 2H25, the company also expects three HASTE missions. HASTE is its hypersonic program, which Rocket Lab expects to "revolutionize" the way missile defense technology is tested in a hypersonic environment.

Then there's Neutron. By the time you're reading this, Launch Complex 3 in Virginia is ready for opening for this reusable medium-lift rocket.

Going forward, the company remains upbeat, which obviously makes sense in light of everything we just discussed. While backlog may be lumpy due to large deals, which is perfectly normal in the defense industry, the company sees backlog acceleration if the initial Neutron launches are successful.

While it won't have a large short-term impact, the company sees up to $155 million in 3Q25 revenue, which would mark 47% growth on top of last year's 55% growth rate. Needless to say, that's impressive.

However, what I care about is a path to profitability? While we don't have very specific comments, we do have comments regarding Rocket Lab's focus on positive free cash flow, thanks to higher operating leverage (margins).

I'm encouraged given the impressive progress made towards neutrons first flight that we're getting closer to moving beyond the past few years of elevated R&D spend and on the path to generating future meaningful operating leverage and positive cash flow.

[...]

So yes, I mean, I think the business could consume -- continue to get some money through 2026. So I would say, more realistically for, I would say, positive free cash flow, 2026. Again, given how aggressively we're moving forward given the demand signals that we're getting, I think that's probably not likely. I think it's much more likely to be in 2027, but it depends. - RKLB 2Q25 Earnings Call

Given the company's growth profile, a potential path to being free cash flow breakeven by 2027 would be mighty impressive.

It would also bode well for shareholders.

Valuation

Needless to say, after rising roughly 9x over the past 14 months, Rocket Lab isn't a hidden gem anymore, especially because it now sports a $20 billion market cap, which is a fifth of Lockheed Martin Corporation's (LMT) market cap.

It also trades at a blended P/S (price-to-sales) ratio of 46.9x, using the FactSet data in the chart below. That's up from 22.1x, when I wrote my prior article in early March. The difference is obviously caused by the 100% gain.

Long-term growth expectations have remained steady since then, as analysts are looking for roughly $2.30 in per-share revenue by 2027. This would imply a long-term P/S ratio of 18.9x.

As a comparison, the Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC) is trading at roughly 2.0x sales. SpaceX is valued at 25.8x 2025 sales. The difference is obviously that Northrop Grumman has mid-single-digit revenue growth, while SpaceX is the private market leader that Rocket Lab is trying to disrupt.

In my prior article, I noted a 19x multiple for SpaceX, which shows that its new funding during the summer substantially boosted its valuation. Given how well RKLB performed, that is no surprise.

That said, applying a 20x multiple to Rocket Lab would imply a $46 fair stock price, roughly $3 above the current price. That's why I stick to a Buy rating, as I believe RKLB has a lot of room to grow beyond 2027, even if stock price gains could be pressured by a consistently falling P/S ratio once the company becomes more mature.

Moreover, RKLB is very prone to volatility and often trades with a high correlation to other profitable growth stocks, making periods where the market is rotating from growth to value a bit challenging for RKLB's market cap.

So, please be careful going forward. While I remain upbeat, this isn't a stock I expect to double again over the next 12 months.

Takeaway

I think we can all agree that Rocket Lab is a phenomenal success story, which is firing on all cylinders with a $1 billion backlog, new strategic acquisitions to expand its footprint in high-demand markets, and a path to positive free cash flow by 2027.

Even better, it's a disruptive, end-to-end provider that's perfectly positioned for the booming space economy and massive defense contracts like Golden Dome, which are critical in a rapidly changing defense environment.

However, the company's 46x sales multiple comes with volatility, as there is little room for error with a valuation like this.

While I remain bullish due to the industry's potential and the fact that RKLB has built a mighty impressive supply chain, I urge investors to be aware of the risks that come with investing in growth stocks.

Enjoy the gains, but be careful.

Risks To My Thesis

On top of general risks like elevated volatility, the company is prone to disruption risks. While it has a clear competitive edge, new entrants are trying to win similar deals and create their own supply chains.

Other major risks are operating risks. If the company were to fail key launches, its customer relationships could be tarnished. One of the reasons why Rocket Lab has grown so fast is its excellent track record.

data.title: Lemonade: The Hidden Gem Of InsurTech
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Lemonade, Inc. (NYSE:LMND), is redefining insurance with an AI-first model that slashes costs, improves transparency, and drives customer loyalty through its socially minded Giveback program. Despite past volatility, the company is now showing real traction with improving loss ratios, climbing product density, and expansion into pet, auto, and European markets, offering massive growth runways. With over $1 billion in in-force premiums and a clear path toward profitability, Lemonade’s tech-driven model positions it far ahead of legacy peers.

Lemonade is Revolutionizing the InsureTech Space of Widest Platform Breadth

Lemonade represents one of the most interesting stories of expansion in the U.S. and European insurtech market. Founded in 2015 and going public in 2020, the firm has revolutionized the consumer model of insurance through a digital-first, AI-driven platform founded upon automation, transparency, and a socially motivated mission.

While the stock has had to withstand unprecedented volatility since its listing, the underlying trend has shifted in Lemonade’s favor. Better unit economics, product growth in high-growth verticals like pet care and telematics-driven auto, and overseas expansion all make a compelling case for building long-term value.

Lemonade is poised on the edge of a structural turning point, which has shown consistent loss-ratio improvement, is holding more premiums through reinsurance management, and will have the ability to leverage wide runways in product lines and geography. The market, based on valuations today, is short-changing the path to sustainable profitability.

Lemonade’s Expanding InsurTech Empire

Lemonade insures renters, homeowners, pets, automobiles, and even life insurance. The company also operates in Europe, alongside the U.S., Germany, the Netherlands, France, and the UK.

Unlike many other insurance companies, Lemonade goes above and beyond in the insurtech industry, employing chatbots and AI to handle customer interactions, policy underwriting, and fraud claims. The company Lemonade also has the “Giveback” program, which allows the policyholders to choose where the company donates the unused premium to charity. This enables the company to differentiate itself from other insurance companies and reinforces its status as a Public Benefit Corporation.

Lemonade has an estimated 2.6 million active users, and the In Force Premiums (IFP) are above $1.07 billion as of the second quarter in 2025, which shows the company is rapidly growing. The company also had one of the most successful IPOs in insurtech, raising $319 million with an issue price of $29 per share. It has also recently acquired Metromile, a U.S. automobile insurance company based on telematics, demonstrating Lemonade’s acceleration of growth into the car insurance sector.

Finally, as Lemonade’s customers age, acquire more assets, and deepen their insurance needs, the company is uniquely positioned to capture a far greater share of wallet, transforming early renters into lifelong, high-value policyholders.

Improving Unit Economics Drives Path to Profitability

Like other insurance companies, Lemonade’s most optimistic scenario is based on improving underwriting discipline while retaining a larger share of the premium economics. Over the year, Lemonade’s trailing-twelve-month gross loss ratio is estimated to have gone down to 67% as of the second quarter of 2025, while it was almost 79% a year ago.

Homeowners insurance’s gross loss ratios have also shown substantial improvement, with gains to 60%. This is also the same with Pets gaining 70% and with Cars gaining 82%. Such improvement explains the financial performance, with a gross profit of $64.3 million.

The company is also focusing on another strategy: serving as the reinsurer for its own Lemonade policies. As of 2025, the level of retention the company was willing to self-insure, and the company fees were 55%. As of July 2025, the company has a 20% retention rate, with hopes that the underwriting has proof of loss. This will increase the company’s revenue and gross profit as all the old contracts expire, allowing for more revenue from IFP contracts and replacing old treaties with new ones. This changes the structure of business economics.

Finally, after cashing in an excess of a billion dollars with a cash flow of $123 million, Lemonade enjoys a massive growth potential in its runway. Management continues to assert its guidance for April 2025, where they expect a positive adjusted free cash flow.

Product Mix Shift Unlocks Long-Term Runways

Although renters insurance initially served as the primary customer acquisition engine, Lemonade’s focus has now shifted to growth opportunities in higher premium categories, such as pet and auto insurance. The combination of structural growth and under-penetration in these markets provides Lemonade with multiple years of compounded growth.

Pet insurance illustrates this dynamic clearly. IFP’s pet insurance business has reached an estimated $350 million, driven by vigorous uptake in the U.S. Industry estimates suggest that in 2024, barely 4% of pets in North America were insured, with the number of insured pets growing at 12% year-over-year. In comparison to markets such as the UK, where nearly 25% of pets are insured, this represents evidence of substantial underinsurance.

Auto insurance, bolstered by the acquisition of Metromile, provides an even greater opportunity. Lemonade has integrated telematics into its underwriting workflow, resulting in nearly a doubling of approval rates compared to the baseline in late 2024. The vehicle business reported its lowest-ever gross loss ratio of 82% in Q2 2025, which is encouraging, though the auto business is inherently unstable. The global usage-based insurance ((UBI) for the automotive market is expected to expand at a 22.9% CAGR from 2024 to 2030, fueled by the proliferation of connected vehicles and growing acceptance of behavior-based pricing by consumers.

Finally, cross-selling is already evident in customer metrics. The average premium per customer increased to $402 in Q2 2025. This change offers a broader range of coverage beyond basic renters insurance. As customers become more experienced, product density should keep rising. This will boost both revenue growth and retention.

International Expansion Provides a Diversified Growth Vector

Lemonade’s operations in Europe remain nascent but burgeoning. The company operates in Germany, France, the Netherlands, and the UK and now has more than 250,000 policyholders. In Q2 2025, in-force premiums in Europe rose more than 200% year-over-year to $43 million. Concurrently, loss ratios also improved 15 points to the low 80s.

The primary European advantage is the regulatory environment. It is more accommodating of faster pricing and underwriting responses than the United States. This would enable AI models to accelerate the feedback loop, which is beneficial for risk selection and enhancing the customer experience. Europe also diversifies Lemonade’s risk, particularly U.S. property catastrophe risk.

Overseas expansion has two primary advantages: the initial premium growth and final risk diversification. Management reaffirmed its confidence by increasing revenue guidance to $710 to $715 million, with adjusted free cash flow expected to stay in the black.

Valuation Mispriced: Why Lemonade’s Premium Is Just the Beginning

LMND, with a ~$3.8 billion market cap, remains a richly valued growth play despite persistent losses. Analyst estimates project narrowing EPS losses from -$2.63 in FY25 to -$1.67 in FY26, alongside revenue acceleration from $713 million to $1.16 billion. At a forward P/S of 3.3x, well below its five-year average of 13.8x, the market appears to be overly discounting execution risks.

Overseas expansion offers dual benefits, premium growth and geographic risk diversification, which could support long-term multiple compression. Management’s raised revenue guidance and positive free cash flow trajectory highlight improving fundamentals, though valuation still prices in ambitious execution with limited margin for error.

This valuation should be put into perspective. Although LMND is currently priced above its conventional peers on sales multiples, traditional insurers are sitting on very different profitability and growth patterns. In comparison to a tech-powered insurer with 29% IFP expansion, loss ratio reduction, and a viable path to achieving positive EBITDA by 2026, the multiple does not seem unjustified today. On continued execution, re-rating to higher P/S multiples or the beginning of profitability-led valuation models seems feasible.

The Risks Investors Can’t Ignore

Although it is progressing, Lemonade does carry a set of risks. Catastrophe exposure is a particular problem, namely in the U.S. homeowners' lines. Significant events or negative prior-period trends can stress loss ratios, and reinsurance costs could increase significantly in pressured markets.

Regulatory restrictions are also tricky. United States rate filings fall behind inflationary patterns, which can result in misalignments between prices and claims. The automotive space, while promising, needs to maintain loss-ratio improvement to justify its economics. Competition against well-established competitors, some of whom have the benefit of scale and capital, will be fierce.

Finally, while Lemonade’s current liquidity position is strong, insurance growth consumes capital. Equity dilution through stock-based compensation has also been a chronic issue, and repeated issuance could keep shareholder returns down.

Takeaway

Lemonade offers investors the rare opportunity to acquire an early-stage disruptor entering profitability. With improved fundamentals, multi-engine growth diversity, and a scalable AI-first platform, LMND has long-term potential exponentially beyond current valuation, providing an attractive bet on transformative winners in fintech and insurance.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Honeywell Takes Quantum Leap As Nvidia Invests $600M
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Nvidia’s VC unit is investing in Honeywell's Quantinuum. (0:15) SoFi launching AI agent ETF. (2:15) Job cuts this year already exceed 2024’s total. (3:07)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) venture capital unit is investing in Honeywell's (NASDAQ:HON) Quantinuum quantum computing unit for the first time.

The investment is part of a $600 million funding round that values the unit at $10 billion. The new funding will help boost Quantinuum's advancement of quantum computing at scale and go towards the launch of Helios, Quantinuum's next-gen quantum computing system.

Honeywell CEO Vimal Kapur said: “We have complete confidence in Quantinuum's ability to continue to lead the quantum revolution and create long-term value for its investors and customers.”

Other quantum computing stocks, such as IonQ (IONQ), Rigetti Computing (RGTI), Arqit Quantum (ARQQ), Quantum Computing (QUBT) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) are higher on the back of the news.

In addition, IonQ announced results of a collaborative research program between the company, AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN), Amazon Web Services (AMZN) and Nvidia to develop and show a quantum-accelerated computational chemistry workflow.

The demonstration focused on a critical step in a Suzuki-Miyaura reaction — a class of chemical transformations used for the synthesis of small molecule drugs. The team achieved more than 20 times improvement in end-to-end time-to-solution compared to previous benchmarks.

And AI dominated active stocks, following weaker-than-expected guidance from Salesforce (CRM), Wall Street analysts said that the prospect and hope of artificial intelligence has not helped the company turn a corner. Yet.

Evercore ISI analyst Kirk Materne said: “While we did not see this quarter as the ‘unlock’ for the bull case, at these levels, we believe stabilization in 2H (constant currency) subs revenue is ‘good enough’ to start shifting sentiment in a more positive direction. That said, seeing is believing and while we believe the risk/reward continues to skew to the upside if thinking out 3 to 4 months, shares are likely in a holding pattern until Dreamforce in mid-Oct.”

C3.ai (AI) is slumping following shaky quarterly results, as it pulled guidance and its founder stepped away from his CEO duties.

KeyBank analyst Eric Heath said, "We significantly lower our revenue estimates for FY26/FY27 … and project large FCF losses through FY28."

Also in the AI space, SoFi Technologies (SOFI) re-entered the exchange-traded fund market with its first launch since 2023, rolling out the SoFi Agentic AI ETF (AGIQ).

AGIQ follows the BITA US Agentic AI Select Index, which tracks U.S.-listed companies engaged in self-driving transportation, AI assistants, cybersecurity, autonomous industrial machinery and enabling technologies such as semiconductors and cloud computing.

And Apple (AAPL) is said to be developing an artificial intelligence-driven web search feature called World Knowledge Answers. It’s expected to launch in spring 2026 as part of a major Siri overhaul, intensifying its competition with OpenAI and Perplexity AI.

Bloomberg says the company has also discussed eventually adding the technology to its Safari web browser and Spotlight, which is used to search from the iPhone home screen.

Looking to the economy, jobs data came in on the soft side ahead of Friday’s payrolls report. Challenger, Gray & Christmas reported a 39% jump in layoffs in August. Total job cuts in 2025 have already exceeded all of 2024 and sit at the highest year-to-date total since 2020.

But services sector activity saw an unexpectedly strong rebound last month. The ISM Non-Manufacturing Index rose to 52 in August from 50.1 in July, exceeding the 50.9 consensus.

Wells Fargo economists say the move was driven by a jump in new orders and “in less uncertain times, jumps in orders are associated with increased demand. Today, it's likely a better reflection of the temporary pull-forward effects around tariffs.”

And in other news of note, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is believed to be seeking to replace -- for the second time -- members of a key CDC panel that makes recommendations on vaccines.

The potential move comes just two weeks before a key meeting of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices is set to meet on Sept. 18 and 19.

Inside Medicine reported that seven new members of the panel have been selected, though they have yet to be formally appointed. The outlet noted that several of the potential new members have spoken out against mRNA vaccines and for ivermectin as a COVID-19 treatment.
